prompt: For a research project titled 'Analysis and Study of Singles Badminton Player Actions Using Sports Videos,' please refine and optimize the following four research components: 1) Object Detection and Tracking within Badminton Videos; 2) Recognition of Technical Actions performed by Singles Players; 3) Recognition of Tactical Intent behind Singles Players' Actions; 4) Prediction of Singles Players' Subsequent Actions.
article:
Assumptions (stated so the recommendations are actionable):  
- Input videos are **standard badminton broadcast** (mostly overhead/behind-court, occasional angle changes, replays, overlays), and the goal is a research pipeline that also generalizes to **fixed-camera training videos**.  
- “Singles player actions” means **per-stroke** technical actions (e.g., smash, drop, net) plus **movement/positioning** and **rally context**; outputs should support coaching-grade analysis (not just clip classification).  
- You can afford a **multi-stage pipeline** (tracking → hit detection → stroke clip extraction → recognition/intent/forecast), but you want it to be robust and evaluable module-by-module.

Below are refined and optimized research components aligned to 2023–2026 evidence and best practices, emphasizing robustness, label efficiency, and clean evaluation.

---

## 1) Object Detection and Tracking within Badminton Videos (players, shuttle, racket, court, pose)

### 1.1 Define the perception outputs as a *court-aligned scene graph*
To avoid brittle downstream models, treat perception as producing a **time-synchronized, court-coordinate scene graph** per frame (or per hit-event window), not just boxes:

**Per frame (or per short window):**
- **Court homography** + keypoints / lines (for coordinate normalization)
- **Player tracks** (ID-consistent boxes + optionally segmentation)
- **Pose keypoints** per tracked player
- **Shuttlecock position** (with visibility/confidence + repaired trajectory)
- **Racket location/pose** (optional but valuable for fine-grained strokes)

This mirrors what successful badminton stroke systems already rely on (pose + shuttle trajectory + court) [[29]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.21085.pdf), and what FineBadminton calls “structural parsing” as the base for higher semantics [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554).

---

### 1.2 Shuttlecock tracking: use badminton-specific tiny-object trackers + explicit trajectory repair
**Why:** shuttlecocks are extremely small, fast, motion-blurred, and often occluded; generic detectors (YOLO-style) are weak here.

**Recommended baseline / core module:** **TrackNetV3** for broadcast badminton shuttle localization [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf), using:
- **Multi-frame heatmap prediction** (learns trajectory cues rather than per-frame appearance only) [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf)  
- **Background-assisted input** via match-level “median-of-rally-medians” to reduce background distractors and ghosting artifacts [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf)  
- **Rectification module** with **repair masks + inpainting** to recover missing segments during occlusion [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf)  
- **Mixup augmentation** for robustness to rare/hard conditions [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf)  

This design is explicitly motivated by shuttlecock speed/blur (smash peak reported at **426 km/h**) and background confusion [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf). The authors provide dataset tooling and implementation details that make it reproducible [[2]](https://github.com/qaz812345/TrackNetV3), and the public shuttle trajectory dataset format is rally-based with visibility flags [[3]](https://hackmd.io/@TUIK/rJkRW54cU).

**Optimization ideas (research-level refinements):**
1. **Track in image space but *filter/regularize in court space*.** Once you have homography, enforce physically plausible motion in court coordinates (piecewise ballistic / smoothness priors) and use the TrackNetV3 rectified output as a strong prior rather than ground truth.  
2. **Add a “rally-aware background model.”** TrackNetV3 already warns about camera angle discrepancy and provides a fallback to rally-median backgrounds [[2]](https://github.com/qaz812345/TrackNetV3). Extend this by maintaining a *camera-angle cluster* and selecting backgrounds per cluster, reducing failures on tournaments with multiple camera rigs.  
3. **Evaluation must include “trajectory completeness,” not only point accuracy.** TrackNetV3’s motivation is exactly that missing segments matter for downstream reasoning (hit detection, intent). TrackNetV3 explicitly targets completeness via rectification [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf).

---

### 1.3 Player detection + Multi-Object Tracking (MOT): use tracking-by-detection with camera motion compensation + robust appearance gating
Badminton singles has only two primary players, but broadcast footage adds:
- camera motion (pan/zoom),  
- frequent partial occlusions (net, umpire chair, overlays),  
- similar jerseys or blurred appearance.

**Recommended MOT design (robust in broadcast):**
- Detector: modern YOLO family / comparable (you can treat as an interchangeable module).
- Tracker: **Deep OC-SORT** for online MOT robustness under camera motion and degraded appearance [[7]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.11813).

Deep OC-SORT’s key robustness mechanisms are directly relevant to broadcast badminton:
- **Camera Motion Compensation (CMC)** integrated into the motion model update pipeline [[7]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.11813)  
- **Dynamic Appearance (DA):** gate appearance embedding updates using detection confidence (reduces corruption from blur/occlusion) [[7]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.11813)  
- **Adaptive Weighting (AW):** increase appearance contribution only when similarity is discriminative (gap between best and second-best match) [[7]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.11813)  

**Why cite sports MOT sources if badminton is 2 players?** Because the same broadcast issues (camera motion + association under blur) are central. SportsMOT explicitly characterizes sports tracking as dominated by **fast/variable motion** and association difficulty, recommending association-sensitive metrics (HOTA/AssA/IDF1) rather than MOTA alone [[5]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2304.05170). Deep OC-SORT is a concrete engineering answer to those failure modes [[7]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.11813).

**Badminton-specific simplification (important):**
- Because singles has 2 players, you can add **strong priors**: “top player / bottom player” identity via homography + feet location, similar to the filtering logic used in skeleton pipelines (keep only skeletons within court; infer top/bottom) [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf).  
- This can outperform generic re-ID in stable overhead views, and it reduces ID switches dramatically.

---

### 1.4 Court line / keypoint detection: heatmap keypoints + homography correction
Court calibration is the foundation for:
- converting player/shuttle positions to a canonical court coordinate system,
- defining “target zones,”
- measuring tactics (pressure, depth, cross-court),
- removing viewpoint variance across venues.

A practical blueprint exists in TennisCourtDetector: predict keypoint heatmaps, then apply **homography-based geometric correction** and optional local refinement; this significantly improved keypoint accuracy in broadcast tennis [[4]](https://github.com/yastrebksv/TennisCourtDetector). The same methodology transfers to badminton because both are planar-court calibration problems.

**Optimization directions:**
- Predict badminton-specific keypoints (service lines, tramlines if visible, net line).  
- Add **temporal smoothing + “re-detect when drift exceeds threshold.”**  
- Use homography consistency as a **self-check**: if predicted court shape is non-physical, downweight and fall back to last stable homography.

---

### 1.5 Pose estimation: prioritize robustness + court filtering; fine-tune if possible
Pose is essential for distinguishing fine-grained strokes with subtle differences (wrist smash vs smash; deceptive net motions). Two relevant signals from sources:

- A badminton-tailored pose estimator improves keypoint localization by modifying YOLOv8-Pose with **Efficient Local Attention (ELA)** and provides a dedicated dataset (4,000 annotated samples) [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12298368/).  
- In badminton stroke recognition research, pose extraction pipelines often use strong general pose estimators (e.g., HRNet for 2D pose) and then filter to on-court players via homography and feet positions [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf).

**Optimization strategy:**
- Use a strong general pose backbone, then do **domain adaptation / fine-tuning** on badminton (even small sets help). The Sensors badminton pose work argues domain data scarcity is a core issue and demonstrates that targeted architectural tweaks + badminton data can materially improve pose accuracy [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12298368/).  
- Always do **court-based person filtering**: spectators/referees are common and will poison skeleton-based models; TemPose explicitly handles this via homography and court inclusion checks [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf).

---

### 1.6 Racket detection: treat as optional but high-value for fine-grained recognition
A dedicated racket dataset (RacketDB) exists and shows why generic datasets are insufficient for badminton racket detection under real-world clutter and motion [[83]](https://www.scitepress.org/Papers/2025/131597/131597.pdf). If you need racket cues:
- Fine-tune a detector on RacketDB-style data [[83]](https://www.scitepress.org/Papers/2025/131597/131597.pdf).  
- Use racket location as an auxiliary cue around hit frames (helps disambiguate “air swings” vs real contact).

---

### 1.7 Perception evaluation protocol: don’t overfit to one metric
Use a layered evaluation stack:

1) **Object detection:** mAP (report mAP50 and mAP@[.5:.95] if possible). RacketDB uses mAP50 plus precision/recall/F1 [[83]](https://www.scitepress.org/Papers/2025/131597/131597.pdf).  
2) **Tracking:** use **HOTA** as primary, with DetA/AssA decomposition and IDF1/IDs/Frag as diagnostics. Sports benchmarks explicitly recommend HOTA and its decomposition [[5]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2304.05170), and HOTA’s rationale is to balance detection+association+localization fairly [[88]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:7b7d0c01-8039-4a47-8683-98863fef1dc1/files/rsf2685357). TrackEval provides standard implementations [[89]](https://github.com/SoccerNet/sn-trackeval).  
3) **Temporal event detection (hit frames / stroke segments):** use temporal mAP over tIoU thresholds as in ActivityNet’s standard protocol (average mAP over tIoU 0.5–0.95) [[92]](http://activity-net.org/challenges/2022/tasks/anet_localization.html) when you treat strokes as temporal segments.

---

## 2) Recognition of Technical Actions performed by Singles Players

### 2.1 Define “technical action” as *hit-centric, multi-label, hierarchical*
A major failure mode in badminton research is treating “action recognition” as clip classification without consistent hit alignment. Instead:

**Technical action unit = a stroke event** anchored at a hit frame, with:
- **Stroke type** (taxonomy)
- **Attributes** (backhand/around-head, height relative to net, etc.)
- **Optional micro-actions** (jump, lunge, split-step) if you want footwork

ShuttleSet provides exactly this stroke-level structure: hit time/frame, player positions, shuttle hitting location, and rich attributes (around-head, backhand, height above/below net) plus 18 shot types [[81]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2306.04948). This supports multi-label learning naturally.

**Hierarchical taxonomy alignment (recommended):**
- Base stroke type labels from ShuttleSet (18 types) [[81]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2306.04948) or VideoBadminton (18 BWF-aligned classes) [[82]](https://www.computer.org/csdl/proceedings-article/bigdata/2024/10825009/23yl5g758wo).  
- Extend upward using FineBadminton’s hierarchy: Foundational actions (primary/subcategories) [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554).  
- This yields a clean mapping from “fine-grained mechanics” to “tactical semantics” later.

---

### 2.2 Temporal problem formulation: event detection → clip extraction → classification (or joint models)
You generally want a two-stage or hybrid approach:

**Stage A — hit event detection / segmentation**
- Detect hit frames (contact moments).  
- A competition-proven approach uses **SwingNet** (MobileNetV2 + BiLSTM) for hit event detection, sometimes with optical-flow-based preprocessing and background removal for salience [[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.10293.pdf), and it is used in CoachAI-style automatic annotation pipelines [[33]](https://github.com/wish44165/A-New-Perspective-for-Shuttlecock-Hitting-Event-Detection).  
- FineBadminton also emphasizes hit-centric processing (“Hit-Centric Keyframe Selection”) for higher-level reasoning [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554).

**Stage B — stroke clip extraction**
- Use a hit-centered clip but avoid fixed windows.  
- BST proposes a **badminton-specific clipping strategy** that includes portions of opponent’s previous and next strokes to capture context and partial future trajectory, avoiding fixed-window tradeoffs [[29]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.21085.pdf). This is particularly important for deceptive strokes where preparation resembles another stroke.

**Stage C — technical action recognition**
Choose one of:
- **Skeleton-based recognition** (strong under background variability)
- **Multimodal pose + shuttle trajectory + court position** (best overall in badminton literature here)

---

### 2.3 Model architecture: multimodal transformers that fuse pose + shuttle + court position
Two key lines of evidence:

**TemPose** (badminton-focused skeleton transformer) shows that:
- Transformers with temporal + interaction layers can model variable-length multi-person actions [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf).  
- Adding **shuttlecock position** and **player court positions** improves fine-grained badminton recognition, and TemPose explicitly defines fusion variants for these modalities [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf).  
- It filters irrelevant people using homography and court inclusion, and uses TrackNet-derived shuttle positions with confidence thresholding [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf).

**BST** argues shuttle trajectory should be a **primary modality**, not a minor auxiliary cue, because player motion can be deceptive while shuttle trajectory reflects the true stroke [[29]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.21085.pdf). BST’s pipeline explicitly combines:
- pose estimation,
- shuttlecock trajectory tracking,
- court line detection / player positioning,  
and reports improved stroke-type classification on multiple datasets [[29]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.21085.pdf). The repository documents normalization practices for shuttle, joints, and court positions, which is critical for stable training [[30]](https://github.com/Va6lue/BST-Badminton-Stroke-type-Transformer).

**Optimized architecture recommendation (research-ready):**
- Backbone: transformer-style temporal modeling.  
- Inputs per stroke clip:
  - 2D skeleton sequence (both players)  
  - shuttle trajectory sequence (x,y + visibility/conf)  
  - player court positions (feet points in court coords)  
  - optional racket position near hit
- Fusion:
  - early fusion for low-level coordination (pose↔shuttle), plus
  - mid-level cross-attention for inter-player interactions (actor/reactor).

This directly follows the strongest badminton-specific architectural evidence in the sources [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf), [[29]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.21085.pdf), [[30]](https://github.com/Va6lue/BST-Badminton-Stroke-type-Transformer).

---

### 2.4 Footwork recognition: treat as *position/pose-derived* unless you have explicit footwork labels
The provided 2023–2026 sources emphasize court position (CP) and skeleton as footwork proxies [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf), and ShuttleSet provides player positions at hit time [[81]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2306.04948). Unless you create a dedicated footwork taxonomy and annotations, a high-leverage approach is:

- Predict **movement primitives** (e.g., split-step timing, lunge direction, recovery trajectory) using pose+feet trajectories as continuous outputs.  
- Use these as auxiliary losses for stroke classification (multi-task learning) to improve generalization.

This is consistent with the movement-forecasting research that models player positions explicitly as part of the rally dynamics [[73]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/25855/25627).

---

### 2.5 Training under limited labels: use assisted labeling + domain adaptation
Badminton labels are expensive; datasets emphasize this explicitly [[81]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2306.04948), and FineBadminton was created because deep semantic labels are otherwise prohibitively costly [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554).

Practical label-efficiency strategy:
1) **Exploit structured datasets (ShuttleSet / ShuttleSet22)** for stroke-level labels and rich metadata [[81]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2306.04948), [[67]](https://arxiv.org/html/2306.15664v1).  
2) Use assisted labeling pipelines:
   - CoachAI-style automatic annotation (hit detection + detection/tracking) [[34]](https://sites.google.com/view/coachai-challenge-2023/),  
   - competition pipelines integrating TrackNet/YOLO/pose [[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.10293.pdf), [[33]](https://github.com/wish44165/A-New-Perspective-for-Shuttlecock-Hitting-Event-Detection).  
3) Use video domain adaptation methods (when moving across venues/cameras):
   - UNITE proposes masked pretraining + collaborative self-training for unsupervised video domain adaptation [[93]](https://cvpr.thecvf.com/virtual/2024/poster/30341).

---

### 2.6 Technical action evaluation: go beyond top-1 accuracy
- For stroke classification: report accuracy/F1 per class (imbalances are common).  
- For hit detection / segmentation: use temporal localization mAP@tIoU (ActivityNet protocol) [[92]](http://activity-net.org/challenges/2022/tasks/anet_localization.html).  
- For shuttle and player position dependence: run ablations like TemPose/BST do (remove shuttle, remove CP, remove opponent) to quantify what truly matters [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf), [[29]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.21085.pdf).

---

## 3) Recognition of Tactical Intent behind Singles Players’ Actions

### 3.1 Operationalize “tactical intent” as a structured layer above technical action
A concrete, modern way to define tactical intent in badminton is exactly FineBadminton’s hierarchy:

- **Foundational Actions** (what stroke / how executed)  
- **Tactical Semantics** (trajectory + strategic intention, e.g., deception, attacking formations)  
- **Decision Evaluation** (quality of choice, rally narrative, pressure continuity) [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554)

FineBadminton explicitly states Tactical Semantics aims to infer strategic intentions like **deceptive plays** and **attacking formations** [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554), and it includes rally-level narratives about offensive–defensive transitions and pressure sequences [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554). This gives you a defensible label design that is consistent with elite coaching language but still machine-learnable.

**Recommended intent label families (singles, stroke-level):**
1) **Pressure state:** apply pressure / relieve pressure / neutral  
2) **Attack vs defense transition:** attack initiation, attack continuation, forced defense, reset  
3) **Target-zone intent:** deep corners, body targeting, front-court tight net, mid-court probing  
4) **Direction intent:** straight vs cross-court (FineBadminton explicitly includes these trajectory semantics) [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554)  
5) **Deception intent:** deceptive vs non-deceptive (FineBadminton explicitly includes deception) [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554)  
6) **Risk/quality intent:** high-risk winner attempt vs safe construction; map to Decision Evaluation [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554)

---

### 3.2 Inputs: intent is not in one frame—use *context windows + opponent state + score*
Intent inference should condition on:
- your own pose + shuttle contact geometry,
- opponent position and movement,
- rally phase, and
- match context (score pressure).

ShuttleSet provides essential context variables per stroke: scores in set, both player locations, shuttle hitting location, and a height-above-net indicator that is explicitly linked to passive vs aggressive returns [[81]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2306.04948). This is a natural bridge from technical action to inferred intent.

FineBadminton’s annotation pipeline also emphasizes structural parsing (tracking + hit detection) as prerequisites to semantic interpretation [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554), reinforcing that intent should be inferred from structured context, not raw pixels alone.

---

### 3.3 Modeling approaches: discriminative, explainable, and causal—use all three (for different research questions)

#### A) Discriminative intent classification (best for accuracy)
- Model: transformer over stroke sequence with multimodal tokens (pose, CP, shuttle, score).  
- Use turn-based structure explicitly (actor/reactor), as stroke forecasting models do [[70]](https://cdn.aaai.org/ojs/20341/20341-13-24354-1-2-20220628.pdf), and as RallyTemPose emphasizes via rally-aware fusion and player embeddings [[71]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024W/CVsports/papers/Ibh_A_Stroke_of_Genius_Predicting_the_Next_Move_in_Badminton_CVPRW_2024_paper.pdf).

#### B) Explainable intent models (best for coaching trust and debugging)
Sports analytics is increasingly moving toward “accurate + explainable.” While PassAI is soccer, it provides a strong blueprint: multimodal prediction plus gradient-based modality contributions and spatial explanations (Grad-CAM) [[47]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.08945). Transfer the idea:

- Provide explanations in badminton as:  
  - “intent is predicted as attack because opponent is deep + shuttle is high + player posture indicates forward acceleration,”  
  - show heatmaps over court zones and key pose joints driving the prediction.

This is especially important because deception is inherently a “model might hallucinate” category; explainability helps validate.

#### C) Causal / counterfactual intent evaluation (best for “what works” questions)
To study whether a tactical intent actually *causes* better outcomes (not just correlates), use causal inference on structured data. A tennis example uses **Double Machine Learning (DML)** to estimate causal effects with heterogeneity (ATE/CATE) across contexts [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12764002/). For badminton, you can analogously estimate:

- causal effect of “attack intent” vs “reset intent” given similar states,  
- causal effect of “cross-court pressure” vs “straight pressure,”  
controlling for player strength, opponent, and score context.

This is the cleanest path to “decision evaluation” as a scientific claim rather than a black-box score.

---

### 3.4 Evaluation for intent recognition: consistency + usefulness, not just accuracy
Because intent labels can be subjective:
- measure **agreement with expert annotation** (accuracy/F1),  
- measure **contextual consistency** (e.g., predicted “attack” should correspond to forward court positioning + downward shuttle trajectories),  
- measure **downstream utility**: does adding intent improve next-action forecasting NLL/CE/MAE (see §4), and does it improve rally outcome prediction?

FineBadminton’s benchmark motivation is exactly that current models struggle with deep tactical comprehension even when they can parse actions [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554), so downstream evaluations are important.

---

## 4) Prediction of Singles Players’ Subsequent Actions (next stroke, landing zone, movement, outcome)

### 4.1 Define forecasting tasks at three levels (so you can compare models fairly)

**Level 1 — Next stroke type (categorical):**  
- Predict next shot type (or top-k distribution).

**Level 2 — Next landing location / shuttle trajectory (continuous distribution):**  
- Predict landing (x,y) or full shuttle path; trajectory prediction work uses ADE/FDE metrics [[85]](https://www.scitepress.org/PublishedPapers/2023/117858/117858.pdf).

**Level 3 — Joint action prediction (best realism):**  
- Predict (shot type, landing (x,y), player recovery movement (x,y)) for multiple future turns.

ShuttleSet22 and CoachAI Track 2 define a canonical version of Level 2/3: given **4 observed strokes**, forecast future strokes’ **shot types + landing coordinates**, with rich context fields including both player and opponent locations [[66]](https://sites.google.com/view/coachai-challenge-2023/tasks/track2), [[67]](https://arxiv.org/html/2306.15664v1).

---

### 4.2 Strong baselines and what to improve

#### A) Transformer sequence forecasting with player style + rally progress (structured data)
**ShuttleNet** is a key baseline: it fuses rally progress and player styles via transformer-based extractors and position-aware fusion, and evaluates shot type with cross-entropy and landing regression with MAE/MSE [[70]](https://cdn.aaai.org/ojs/20341/20341-13-24354-1-2-20220628.pdf). It explicitly treats the future as stochastic and uses **best-of-K sampling** (K=10) for evaluation [[70]](https://cdn.aaai.org/ojs/20341/20341-13-24354-1-2-20220628.pdf). This matches the CoachAI evaluation philosophy.

**CoachAI Challenge Track 2** formalizes multi-sample forecasting:
- models must output **6 sampled predictions per instance**, and evaluation uses cross-entropy for shot type and MAE for landing coordinates, selecting the closest sample to ground truth [[66]](https://sites.google.com/view/coachai-challenge-2023/tasks/track2), [[69]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/1042.pdf).  
This evaluation protocol is a practical “multi-hypothesis” setup (even if it doesn’t call it minADE-k explicitly).

**Optimization directions:**
- Don’t just predict a mean landing point; output multi-modal distributions (see ShuttleFlow below).  
- Add explicit opponent conditioning (many models already include it; ensure it’s ablated).

#### B) Movement forecasting with interaction graphs (structured data)
DyMF models player interactions using a Player Movements graph and hierarchical fusion, predicting future strokes including both players’ locations and shot types [[73]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/25855/25627). This is important because singles tactics are tightly coupled to movement and recovery; if you only predict shot type/location, you miss the “why” and the counterplay.

**Optimization directions:**
- Combine DyMF-style interaction modeling with shuttle trajectory tokens from TrackNetV3 [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf) to improve physical plausibility.

#### C) Pose-informed forecasting (video-derived, richer motion)
RallyTemPose (CVPRW 2024) predicts next moves using **court position + skeleton pose** and player embeddings, with rally-aware fusion blocks to incorporate strategic/technical considerations [[71]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024W/CVsports/papers/Ibh_A_Stroke_of_Genius_Predicting_the_Next_Move_in_Badminton_CVPRW_2024_paper.pdf). This is a key research direction: forecasting should not be only discrete strokes; it should condition on *how* the player is moving.

**Optimization directions:**
- Use hit-centric windows (as FineBadminton suggests) [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554).  
- Add uncertainty modeling rather than single deterministic outputs.

#### D) Probabilistic forecasting with normalizing flows (multi-modal outcomes)
**ShuttleFlow** reframes forecasting as **distribution learning** over next shot type + shuttle position using **conditional normalizing flows**, explicitly motivated by the multi-modality of badminton outcomes and the inadequacy of bivariate normal assumptions [[74]](https://d-nb.info/1364929597/34). It trains via negative log-likelihood (NLL) and samples diverse hypotheses with Poisson disk sampling to reduce redundancy [[74]](https://d-nb.info/1364929597/34).

This is the most direct match to real singles badminton: at many states, there are several viable shots.

**Optimization directions:**
- Evaluate calibration and diversity (not only “closest-of-K”).  
- Condition on player identity and opponent identity (ShuttleFlow conditions on players and preceding shots) [[74]](https://d-nb.info/1364929597/34).

#### E) Hierarchical intent → action generation (simulation-grade realism)
**RallyNet** models badminton as a contextual MDP where the agent first selects a **context as intent** (Experiential Context Selector), then generates actions, and uses **Geometric Brownian Motion** as an inductive bias for interactions [[76]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2403.12406). It evaluates realism via rally duration realism and outcome consistency, including win-rate alignment to real data [[76]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2403.12406), [[77]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.12406v1).

This is the cleanest blueprint for “intent leads to action”:
- tactical intent is a latent variable (context),  
- action generation depends on opponent responses,  
- you can run counterfactual simulations (“what if player chose a reset instead of attack?”).

---

### 4.3 Video-to-forecast pipeline: ensure structured extraction is reliable
Forecasting models assume structured input. CoachAI-style work explicitly builds pipelines for:
- rally trimming,
- player/court keypoint detection,
- shuttle direction prediction,
- hit-frame detection [[78]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2307.16000).

You should treat this as a first-class research module and quantify its errors, because perception noise will dominate forecasting otherwise.

Also note an applied badminton shot extraction system shows that fusing shuttle tracking and swing/action detection with a refinement stage can drastically improve shot extraction accuracy and F1 compared to TrackNet alone [[84]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11244353/). This is direct evidence that “pipeline glue” matters.

---

### 4.4 Forecast evaluation: match metrics to uncertainty and multi-step rollout
From sources, the established badminton forecasting metrics include:
- **Cross-entropy** for shot type prediction [[70]](https://cdn.aaai.org/ojs/20341/20341-13-24354-1-2-20220628.pdf), [[69]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/1042.pdf)  
- **MAE/MSE** for landing coordinate prediction [[70]](https://cdn.aaai.org/ojs/20341/20341-13-24354-1-2-20220628.pdf), [[69]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/1042.pdf)  
- **Best-of-K sampling** evaluation (ShuttleNet K=10; CoachAI K=6) [[70]](https://cdn.aaai.org/ojs/20341/20341-13-24354-1-2-20220628.pdf), [[69]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/1042.pdf)  
- **NLL** for density modeling in normalizing flows [[74]](https://d-nb.info/1364929597/34)  
- **ADE/FDE** for trajectory forecasting [[85]](https://www.scitepress.org/PublishedPapers/2023/117858/117858.pdf)

**Optimized metric suite (recommended):**
1) **One-step and multi-step** metrics separately (multi-step compounds errors).  
2) Report:
   - CE for shot type; MAE/MSE for landing;  
   - NLL when you output proper distributions (ShuttleFlow) [[74]](https://d-nb.info/1364929597/34);  
   - best-of-K metrics for multi-hypothesis settings (CoachAI) [[69]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/1042.pdf);  
   - ADE/FDE for full trajectory prediction tasks [[85]](https://www.scitepress.org/PublishedPapers/2023/117858/117858.pdf).  
3) Add association with realism:
   - rally length distribution error,  
   - win-rate alignment / outcome consistency (RallyNet-style) [[76]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2403.12406), [[77]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.12406v1).

---

## Practical “refinement checklist” (ties the 4 components together)

### A) Standardize everything into court coordinates early
- Court homography → map player feet, shuttle positions, and target zones into a canonical court frame (TemPose already uses homography for court filtering) [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf).  
- This is the single biggest improvement for cross-venue generalization and tactical interpretability.

### B) Make the pipeline hit-centric
- Hit detection (SwingNet-style) [[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.10293.pdf) → stroke clip extraction (BST strategy) [[29]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.21085.pdf) → technical action → tactical semantics → forecasting.  
- FineBadminton’s baseline strategies reinforce hit-centric reasoning for deep semantics [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554).

### C) Build explicit robustness for broadcast artifacts
- Camera motion: CMC in tracking (Deep OC-SORT) [[7]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.11813)  
- Occlusion: shuttle trajectory rectification/inpainting (TrackNetV3) [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf)  
- Background distraction: match median background (TrackNetV3) [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf)  
- Blur: practical systems in broadcast tracking challenges even use deblurring preprocessing [[10]](https://hal.science/hal-04462235/document) (transferable idea)

### D) Evaluate modularly to avoid “mystery gains”
Adopt benchmark design principles seen in SoccerNet Tracking: separate association-only vs full detection+association when diagnosing tracking [[90]](https://www.soccer-net.org/tasks/tracking), [[91]](https://eval.ai/web/challenges/challenge-page/1539/overview). For badminton:
- Evaluate shuttle tracking alone (TrackNetV3 metrics) [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf)  
- Evaluate hit detection alone (temporal mAP protocol) [[92]](http://activity-net.org/challenges/2022/tasks/anet_localization.html)  
- Evaluate stroke classification with/without shuttle and with/without opponent inputs (TemPose/BST-style ablations) [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf), [[29]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.21085.pdf)  
- Evaluate forecasting with oracle vs predicted perception to quantify error propagation.

### E) Use datasets according to the semantic layer you want
- **Perception (shuttle):** Shuttlecock Trajectory Dataset [[3]](https://hackmd.io/@TUIK/rJkRW54cU) + TrackNetV3 codebase [[2]](https://github.com/qaz812345/TrackNetV3)  
- **Technical stroke + context:** ShuttleSet stroke records [[81]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2306.04948); ShuttleSet22 for standardized forecasting splits and CoachAI evaluation [[67]](https://arxiv.org/html/2306.15664v1), [[69]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/1042.pdf)  
- **Video clip action recognition:** VideoBadminton for clip-level fine-grained recognition [[82]](https://www.computer.org/csdl/proceedings-article/bigdata/2024/10825009/23yl5g758wo)  
- **Hierarchical tactic/intent semantics:** FineBadminton tactical semantics + decision evaluation [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554)  
- **Racket:** RacketDB [[83]](https://www.scitepress.org/Papers/2025/131597/131597.pdf)

---

## Summary of optimized component designs (one-page view)

1) **Object detection & tracking**
- Shuttle: TrackNetV3 (background + rectification + mixup) [[1]](https://people.cs.nycu.edu.tw/~yushuen/data/TrackNetV3.pdf)  
- Players: detection + Deep OC-SORT (CMC + dynamic appearance + adaptive weighting) [[7]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.11813)  
- Court: keypoint heatmaps + homography correction (TennisCourtDetector-style) [[4]](https://github.com/yastrebksv/TennisCourtDetector)  
- Pose: badminton-adapted pose estimation (YOLOv8-Pose+ELA) or strong general pose + court filtering [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12298368/), [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf)  
- Evaluate: HOTA via TrackEval for tracking [[88]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:7b7d0c01-8039-4a47-8683-98863fef1dc1/files/rsf2685357), [[89]](https://github.com/SoccerNet/sn-trackeval); temporal mAP for events [[92]](http://activity-net.org/challenges/2022/tasks/anet_localization.html)

2) **Technical action recognition**
- Define strokes as hit-centric multi-label events (type + around-head/backhand + height + optional footwork) using ShuttleSet schema [[81]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2306.04948)  
- Pipeline: hit detection [[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.10293.pdf) → BST clip extraction [[29]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.21085.pdf) → multimodal transformer (pose + shuttle + court) (TemPose/BST direction) [[25]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2023W/CVSports/papers/Ibh_TemPose_A_New_Skeleton-Based_Transformer_Model_Designed_for_Fine-Grained_Motion_CVPRW_2023_paper.pdf), [[29]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.21085.pdf)  
- Train with ablations and domain adaptation when transferring venues [[93]](https://cvpr.thecvf.com/virtual/2024/poster/30341)

3) **Tactical intent recognition**
- Operationalize via FineBadminton’s Tactical Semantics + Decision Evaluation layers (deception, formations, pressure, transitions) [[44]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.07554)  
- Model using contextual multimodal sequences; add explainability (PassAI-style multimodal explanations adapted to badminton) [[47]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.08945)  
- For “what works” questions, add causal estimation (DML-style approach demonstrated in tennis) [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12764002/)

4) **Subsequent action prediction**
- Structured forecasting: ShuttleNet-style player style + rally progress with multi-sample best-of-K evaluation [[70]](https://cdn.aaai.org/ojs/20341/20341-13-24354-1-2-20220628.pdf)  
- Competition-grade: CoachAI Track 2 (K=6, CE + MAE) [[69]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/1042.pdf)  
- Better uncertainty: ShuttleFlow normalizing flows with NLL + diverse sampling [[74]](https://d-nb.info/1364929597/34)  
- Realism + intent-to-action: RallyNet hierarchical context(intent) → actions + interaction priors [[76]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2403.12406), [[77]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.12406v1)  
- Movement interaction: DyMF graph forecasting of both players’ locations + shot types [[73]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/25855/25627)

If you implement these four components with strict court-coordinate normalization, hit-centric segmentation, multi-hypothesis uncertainty, and modular evaluation, you will have a research pipeline that is both state-of-the-art-aligned and scientifically defensible end-to-end.
